---
title: "MD7115 Part 3"
subtitle: "Network Analysis in R"
author: '<br><br>Xiaotao Shen <br> Assistant Professor <br> <a href="http://shen-lab.org"> <i class="fa fa-home fa-fw"></i>&nbsp; shen-lab.org</a><br> <a href="mailto:xiaotao.shen@ntu.edu.sg"><i class="fa fa-paper-plane fa-fw"></i>&nbsp; xiaotao.shen@ntu.edu.sg</a><br>'
institute: "Lee Kong Chian School of Medicine, NTU Singapore"
date: '`r Sys.Date()`'
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: [xaringan-themer.css, my-theme.css]
    self_contained: true
    nature:
      ratio: '16:9'
      countdown: false
      titleSlideClass: [center, middle]
      countIncrementalSlides: false
    seal: true
    yolo: false
---

layout: true
background-image: url(images/lkc_logo.png)
background-position: 100% 0%
background-size: 20%

<div class="my-footer"><span>Xiaotao Shen/www.shen-lab.org</span></div>

---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  eval = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  cache = FALSE,
  dpi = 72
)
```

# Part 3 goals

In this section, we will:

1. Read node and edge tables
2. Build a graph object
3. Compute simple node properties
4. Compare layouts
5. Produce one interpretable network plot

---

# Packages

```{r}
library(readr)
library(tidyverse)
library(igraph)
library(tidygraph)
library(ggraph)
library(ggsci)
library(shadowtext)
```

---

# Data for the live demo

We will use two local files:

- `2_demo_data/example_node_data.csv`
- `2_demo_data/example_edge_data.csv`

These represent a ready-to-plot biological network.

---

# Read node data

```{r}
node_data <- readr::read_csv("2_demo_data/example_node_data.csv")
head(node_data)
```

What to check:

- one row per node
- a unique node identifier
- useful attributes for color, size, or labels

---

# Read edge data

```{r}
edge_data <- readr::read_csv("2_demo_data/example_edge_data.csv")
head(edge_data)
```

What to check:

- `from` and `to` columns match node IDs
- edge weights are numeric
- missing values are handled

---

# Minimal workflow

```text
node table + edge table
        ->
graph object
        ->
metrics and attributes
        ->
layout
        ->
visualization
```

---

# Build a graph object

```{r}
graph_data <-
  tidygraph::tbl_graph(
    nodes = node_data,
    edges = edge_data,
    directed = FALSE
  )

graph_data
```

---

# Add degree

```{r}
graph_data <-
  graph_data %>%
  dplyr::mutate(degree = tidygraph::centrality_degree())

graph_data
```

Interpretation:

- larger degree often means more connected
- not automatically more important biologically

---

# Basic plot

```{r}
ggraph(graph_data, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_node_point(size = 3, color = "#2c7fb8") +
  theme_void()
```

This is often the first quick quality-control plot.

---

# Add node size and color

```{r}
ggraph(graph_data, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_node_point(aes(size = degree, fill = class), shape = 21) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_graph()
```

---

# Add labels selectively

```{r}
ggraph(graph_data, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_node_point(aes(size = degree, fill = class), shape = 21) +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = ifelse(degree >= 3, mol_name, NA)), repel = TRUE, size = 3) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_graph()
```

Only label what helps interpretation.

---

# Compare layouts

```{r}
ggraph(graph_data, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_node_point(aes(size = degree, fill = class), shape = 21) +
  theme_graph()
```

```{r}
ggraph(graph_data, layout = "circle") +
  geom_edge_arc(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_node_point(aes(size = degree, fill = class), shape = 21) +
  theme_graph()
```

---

# Layout choice

Choose layout based on the question:

- `fr`: general-purpose exploratory plot
- `circle`: emphasizes ordering or grouping
- `bipartite`: two node types
- `manual`: best when you want full control

---

# Example of a manual strategy

In some biological datasets, we want different classes to occupy different regions.

Examples:

- wearable versus omics
- host versus microbiome
- source versus target

That is a visualization decision, not a new analysis method.

---

# Final network plot

```{r}
plot <-
  ggraph(graph_data, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(width = abs(lagged_cor), color = lagged_cor), alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_node_point(aes(fill = class, size = degree), shape = 21, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = ifelse(degree >= 3, mol_name, NA), color = class), repel = TRUE, size = 3) +
  scale_edge_width_continuous(range = c(0.2, 1.5)) +
  scale_edge_color_gradient2(low = "#3B4992FF", mid = "white", high = "#EE0000FF") +
  theme_graph()

plot
```

---

# Optional extension: modules

```{r}
graph_data <-
  graph_data %>%
  dplyr::mutate(module = as.factor(group_louvain()))
```

Possible uses:

- functional clustering
- pathway-level interpretation
- picking representative labels

---

# Second network example

A GO similarity network can be built from:

1. enriched GO terms
2. pairwise semantic similarity
3. thresholded edge list
4. graph construction
5. community detection

We will not live-code the full pipeline today, but this is the same network logic.

---

# What matters most

- define nodes clearly
- define edges clearly
- check ID consistency
- choose layout for readability
- label sparingly
- interpret the biology, not only the aesthetics

---

# Transition

We now switch from general networks to trees.

Question:

**What changes when the structure is hierarchical instead of general?**
